bins.name: FERC interconnection authority
bins.desc: EPSA argues that FPA Section 212(h) does not constrain FERC's jurisdiction over large load interconnection because interconnection service is legally separate from wheeling or retail delivery. FERC's authority rests on Sections 210 and 211, and Section 212(h) arguments are a pretext for transmission owners to control or delay interconnections.
quotes: These arguments incorrectly conflate the wheeling/delivery and interconnection components of transmission service.
the Commission's authority to require those types of interconnections arises under separate statutory provisions – FPA Sections 210 and 211.

The arguments raising section 212(h) concerns attempt to establish a retail "piece" of the connection which then grants transmission owners a blanket lever for control over the interconnection – a scenario which, based on the ANOPR's reasoning and the additional legal support included in EPSA's initial comments, runs contrary to the statutory intent and the goal of the ANOPR itself.

This hook is also inappropriately established by comments suggesting that any hybrid load interconnection must include a delivery meter – thereby transmogrifying every connection into a state-jurisdictional arrangement.

bins.name: Hybrid load delivery meter dispute
bins.desc: EPSA opposes the view that any hybrid load interconnection must include a delivery meter. Requiring such a meter would treat every hybrid connection as a state-jurisdictional arrangement and give transmission owners unwarranted control over federal interconnection.
quotes: This hook is also inappropriately established by comments suggesting that any hybrid load interconnection must include a delivery meter – thereby transmogrifying every connection into a state-jurisdictional arrangement.

bins.name: Prioritize co-located load guidance
bins.desc: EPSA urges FERC to act first on co-located large load arrangements by drawing on the extensive PJM show cause record. This guidance can and should proceed separately from the broader rulemaking and must not disrupt ongoing commercial transactions.
quotes: EPSA urges the Commission to move forward with direction and guidance for co-located arrangements based on the extensive record set out in the PJM Show Cause proceeding on these issues.

issuing guidance for co-located load arrangements should be task one in the near term for the Commission. This action does not need to run concurrently with any next steps in this rulemaking proceeding

EPSA continues to urge the Commission to ensure that consideration and development of the next step in the instant rulemaking proceeding does not delay, hamper, or interfere with ongoing commercial transactions and arrangements.

bins.name: Preserve competitive markets
bins.desc: EPSA strongly opposes proposals that would allow transmission owners to build and rate-base new generation, arguing this would raise consumer costs, shift risk to ratepayers, and reverse two decades of competitive market policy. Any reform should rest on market-based mechanisms rather than reregulation.
quotes: this proceeding is being utilized as an opportunity to press unwarranted attacks on our nation's competitive power markets in an attempt to hijack this critical emergent concern to dismantle competitive mechanisms and allow monopolistic behavior to flourish further.
Fundamentally, monopoly ownership stifles competition, increases consumer costs, distorts markets, and unfairly shifts financial risk from investors to ratepayers.

Allowing transmission owners to return to the business of building and operating rate-based generation after Order No. 2000 was established to resolve massive cost overruns and poor operating performance under the previous regime should not be part of any solution.
In this uncertain environment, competitive markets place the risk of investment to meet load growth on shareholders rather than ratepayers.

A reliance on innovative market-based mechanisms should be the foundation of any next step in this rulemaking proceeding, while plaintive calls to reregulate should be dismissed for what they are – self-serving mis-directions which will not help us achieve our national goals.

bins.name: PJM show cause record
bins.desc: EPSA references the PJM show cause proceeding as the foundation for near-term co-located load guidance, citing its extensive record developed over more than nine months.
quotes: EPSA urges the Commission to move forward with direction and guidance for co-located arrangements based on the extensive record set out in the PJM Show Cause proceeding on these issues.

bins.name: Phase guidance before rulemaking
bins.desc: EPSA recommends a phased approach: issue co-located load guidance promptly from the existing record, then address broader large load interconnection rules. The two tracks should run independently so pending commercial arrangements are not disrupted.
quotes: EPSA urges the Commission to move forward with direction and guidance for co-located arrangements based on the extensive record set out in the PJM Show Cause proceeding on these issues.

issuing guidance for co-located load arrangements should be task one in the near term for the Commission. This action does not need to run concurrently with any next steps in this rulemaking proceeding

EPSA continues to urge the Commission to ensure that consideration and development of the next step in the instant rulemaking proceeding does not delay, hamper, or interfere with ongoing commercial transactions and arrangements.

bins.name: Block utility self-build of generation
bins.desc: EPSA characterizes calls for transmission owners to build rate-based generation as self-serving attacks on competitive markets. Historical examples from PJM and Georgia illustrate that ratepayers bear the cost of overbuilt or stranded utility investments, while competitive markets place that risk on investors.
quotes: this proceeding is being utilized as an opportunity to press unwarranted attacks on our nation's competitive power markets in an attempt to hijack this critical emergent concern to dismantle competitive mechanisms and allow monopolistic behavior to flourish further.
Fundamentally, monopoly ownership stifles competition, increases consumer costs, distorts markets, and unfairly shifts financial risk from investors to ratepayers.

Allowing transmission owners to return to the business of building and operating rate-based generation after Order No. 2000 was established to resolve massive cost overruns and poor operating performance under the previous regime should not be part of any solution.
In this uncertain environment, competitive markets place the risk of investment to meet load growth on shareholders rather than ratepayers.

A reliance on innovative market-based mechanisms should be the foundation of any next step in this rulemaking proceeding, while plaintive calls to reregulate should be dismissed for what they are – self-serving mis-directions which will not help us achieve our national goals.

bins.name: Section 212(h) does not limit FERC
bins.desc: EPSA rebuts arguments that FPA Section 212(h) limits FERC's authority over large load interconnection. The Commission's interconnection power derives from Sections 210 and 211, which are independent of the wheeling and retail delivery provisions addressed by Section 212(h).
quotes: These arguments incorrectly conflate the wheeling/delivery and interconnection components of transmission service.
the Commission's authority to require those types of interconnections arises under separate statutory provisions – FPA Sections 210 and 211.

The arguments raising section 212(h) concerns attempt to establish a retail "piece" of the connection which then grants transmission owners a blanket lever for control over the interconnection – a scenario which, based on the ANOPR's reasoning and the additional legal support included in EPSA's initial comments, runs contrary to the statutory intent and the goal of the ANOPR itself.

This hook is also inappropriately established by comments suggesting that any hybrid load interconnection must include a delivery meter – thereby transmogrifying every connection into a state-jurisdictional arrangement.
